content.core_argument: The cost of waiting for a 'better entry' in a wide-moat compounder is historically higher than the cost of overpaying at initiation. Costco has compounded at ~20%/yr for 20 years while value investors waited for a cheaper entry that never came. The business is structurally harder to disrupt than the bear acknowledges: membership renewal at 92.9% is not a sign of peak optionism — it is a lagging indicator of deep customer entrenchment that takes years to erode. At a 10-year horizon, an investor entering at 48x P/E in a business growing earnings at ~12%/yr gets to ~24x P/E after 5 years even with zero multiple expansion. The multiple risk is real but manageable at a decade horizon.
content.cost_of_underexposure_case: If Costco compounds earnings at 12%/yr for 10 years (consistent with its 10-year history), the stock at 40x terminal P/E (a moderate de-rating) would be worth ~$2,400 — a 2.5x from today. An investor who waits for a 20% pullback to $760 and never gets it sacrifices that entire return. The expected value of waiting is negative when the business quality is this high and the catalyst for a pullback is speculative. The 'wait for mean reversion' strategy has destroyed more wealth in quality compounders than premature entry ever did.
rebuttals_to_conservative: Conservative will argue DCF base case shows 44% downside — but that DCF uses a 3% terminal growth rate, which is below the last 5-year FCF CAGR of 14.6%. Applying a growth-adjusted terminal rate is not aggressive; it is consistent with the company's actual track record.

Conservative will argue the risk-free rate at 4.3% makes equities generally unattractive — but Costco's earnings yield is indeed only 2.1% vs 4.3% risk-free, which is a fair concern. However, the earnings yield grows at 12%/yr, so by year 5 the earnings yield on cost (~3.3%) will exceed the risk-free rate assuming no multiple expansion.

Conservative will argue waiting for a pullback provides better risk/reward — but Costco's drawdowns are shallow compared to the market: max drawdown in the last 10 years was ~25% (COVID crash), while the S&P 500 drew down 34%. You get a smaller pullback than you hope for in quality businesses.
rebuttals_to_neutral: Neutral's staged-entry approach assumes pullbacks occur predictably — but Costco has spent the majority of the last 10 years at or near all-time highs, meaning Stage 2 and Stage 3 tranches may never get triggered, leaving the investor underweight a compounder for years.

Dollar-cost-averaging into a quality compounder adds administrative complexity and emotional decision points (do I still add at the tranche trigger?) without meaningfully improving the 10-year outcome vs a full position today.

citations.claim: Max drawdown COVID March 2020: COST ~-25%; S&P 500 ~-34%
citations.url_or_id: COST and SPY adjusted close Feb-Mar 2020
